originalTitle: Inside the alleged Russian operation to trigger anti-government protests in Angola
title: A Big Story from Angola: People in Court!
content:
Hello from Africa!

Hello, amazing friends! Today, we have a special news story for you from a wonderful country in Africa. It's called Angola. Can you say that? An-go-la! Angola is a big, beautiful country on the western side of Africa. It has sunny beaches where the ocean waves crash, and big, grassy lands where amazing animals live, like elephants and zebras! It's a place full of friendly people, delicious food, and exciting stories. Today, we're going to talk about something happening there right now, in a special place called a court.

What is a Protest?

Sometimes, people want to tell their leaders or the government something important. Maybe they want better schools for children, or clean water for everyone to drink, or safe roads for cars and bikes. When many people gather together to say what they think, that is called a protest. Imagine if you and all your friends wanted to tell your teacher that you really, really wanted more time to play outside. You might all stand together and say, "More playtime! More playtime!" That's a bit like a protest, but on a much bigger scale! Protests are usually peaceful. People might hold up colorful signs with words on them, or sing songs to share their ideas, or just stand together to show how many people feel the same way. It's a way for people to use their voices to ask for changes. Did you know? Angola is home to the giant sable antelope! This beautiful animal has very long, curved horns, and you can only find it in Angola!

Who Are the People in Our Story?

In Angola, there's a group of people who are part of this story. Some of these people are from Angola itself, the country we're talking about. And some of them are from a country very far away, called Russia. They are like neighbors, but from different parts of the world, coming together in Angola. This group of people is going to a special place in Angola. They are going to a court because someone has made an important accusation about them. An accusation means someone is saying they did something, but it hasn't been proven true yet.

What Are They Accused Of?

So, what is this group of Angolan and Russian people being accused of? People are saying that this group tried to make other Angolans angry. They are accused of trying to make many people gather and protest against their own government. It's like someone saying you tried to make all your classmates upset with your teacher, to make them all complain together. But here's a very important part of the story: The group of people from Angola and Russia say, "No! We did not do that." They say that what they are being accused of is not true. They deny it.

What Happens in a Court?

When people are accused of doing something, and they say they didn't do it, they go to a special place called a court. A court is like a big, serious classroom where everyone listens very carefully to find out the truth. In the court, there is a very important person called a judge. The judge is like a very wise teacher or a trusted grown-up who makes sure everyone follows the rules. They listen to all sides of the story very carefully. In court, different people tell their stories. Some people will say what they think happened, explaining why they made the accusation. Then, the group of Angolan and Russian people will also get to tell their side of the story. They will explain why they say they didn't do what they are accused of. This whole process of listening to everyone and trying to find out the truth is called a "trial." Did you know? In many courts around the world, the judge has a special wooden hammer called a gavel! They tap it to say "Order, order!" to make sure everyone is quiet and listening.

Why Do We Have Trials?

Trials are very, very important because they help make sure things are fair for everyone. Imagine if someone just said you did something wrong, and you immediately got in trouble without being able to say anything! That wouldn't be fair, right? That's why trials are so important. Everyone has a chance to tell their side of the story. No one is called "guilty" (which means they definitely did something wrong) until all the facts are heard, and the judge makes a decision. This special rule is called being "innocent until proven guilty." It's a big rule that helps make sure everyone is treated fairly and kindly.

What Will Happen Next?

So, in Angola, these Angolan and Russian people will go to court. They will tell their story to the judge. The people who made the accusation will tell their story too. The judge will listen to every single word, every piece of information, and think very, very hard. Then, after hearing everything, the judge will decide what is true. This decision will help everyone understand what really happened. Did you know? Africa is the second-largest continent in the world! It has 54 different countries, each with its own special cultures and stories, just like Angola!

Why is This Story Important?

This story from Angola reminds us that rules are important in every country, just like rules are important in your home or at school. It also shows us that if someone is accused of something, they always get a chance to speak and explain themselves. It's about listening to everyone and trying to find the truth, fairly. Just like when you and a friend might have a disagreement, and a grown-up helps you both talk it out to understand what happened. Countries need to be strong and fair for everyone to live happily and safely.

A Fair Ending

So, that's the big story from Angola right now. A group of people from Angola and Russia are going to court. They are accused of trying to start protests, but they say, "No, we didn't do it!" The court and the judge will help everyone find out what really happened. We hope for fairness and truth for everyone in Angola, and we send our best wishes for peace and understanding in this wonderful African country.
summary: In Angola, a group of people from Angola and Russia are going to court because they are accused of trying to make people protest against the government, but they say they didn't do it.
whyMatters: This story matters because it shows how important rules and fairness are in a country, and that everyone gets a chance to tell their side of the story in court to find out the truth.

originalTitle: Israeli settlers vandalise school, raise Israeli flag in occupied West Bank
title: Sad News from a School Far Away: Let's Keep Schools Safe!
content:
Hello, Wonderful Learners!

Hi everyone! Your friendly news reporter is here from Africa. I have a story to tell you today, and it’s about a school far, far away. It’s a bit of a sad story, but it also teaches us something important about kindness and how special schools are.

Imagine your school. You go there to learn new things, right? You play with your friends, you draw pictures, you learn to read and count. Your school is a happy place where you grow your brain and make good memories.

A Mess at a School in Huwara

But something not-so-nice happened at a boys’ school in a place called Huwara. Huwara is a village a long way from Africa, but it’s still a place where children go to school, just like you.

Some grown-ups, who live nearby and are called Israeli settlers, did something that made people sad. They went to the school and made a big mess. This is called vandalism.

They broke some things, and they drew on the walls. They also put up a flag that wasn't the school's flag. Imagine if someone came to your school and did that! How would you feel?

What is Vandalism?

Vandalism is when someone spoils or damages something that belongs to other people. It's like drawing on someone's book without asking, or breaking a toy that isn’t yours.

It’s not a kind thing to do because it hurts feelings and makes places sad. Schools are for learning and playing, not for messing up.

Why Schools are So Special

Schools are like magic places! They help children grow super smart. You learn about animals, stars, and history. You learn how to write letters and stories. You learn to do sums and count all your fingers and toes!

More than just learning facts, schools help you learn to be a good friend. You learn to share your crayons, to take turns on the swing, and to help someone who is sad.

When a school is messy or broken, it makes it hard for children to learn and play. It makes everyone feel a bit worried and upset.

Did you know? Schools have been helping children learn for thousands of years! People always knew how important it was for kids to get smart.

How the Children Felt

When the children in Huwara saw their school messed up, they must have felt very sad. Maybe confused, or even a little scared. A school should be a safe place where children feel happy.

Grown-ups are supposed to protect children and their schools, not make things messy or scary. Everyone wants children to be safe and happy, especially at school.

Did you know? Every child in the world has a right to go to school and learn. It’s like a special promise for all kids!

Learning Kindness and Talking

Sometimes, grown-ups have problems or disagree about things. But breaking things and making a mess is never a good way to solve a problem.

The best way is to talk. To use kind words and try to understand each other. Just like you learn to share your toys, grown-ups can learn to share ideas and find ways to live happily together.

When we are kind to each other, our hearts feel warm. When we are unkind, it makes everyone feel cold and sad.

What Happens Next?

We hope that the school in Huwara will be cleaned up very quickly. We hope that the broken things will be fixed. We want the children to go back to their classrooms and learn and play without worry.

We hope that all the grown-ups who live near Huwara will remember how important schools are. And that they will work together to keep all children safe and happy in their learning places.

Did you know? When we learn new things, our brains grow stronger, just like our muscles when we play outside! Learning helps us make good choices too.

A Message of Hope and Friendship

From your friends here in Africa, we send our warmest wishes to the children of Huwara. We believe that peace and kindness are the best ways for everyone to live. We hope everyone remembers to be kind and protect the special places where children learn.

Let's all promise to be kind to our schools and to each other. Because when we learn and play together, the world becomes a much happier place!

Thank you for listening, wonderful learners!
summary: Some grown-ups made a big mess at a boys' school in a place called Huwara, which made children sad because schools are important for learning and friendship.
whyMatters: It matters because schools are super important places where children learn, make friends, and grow up smart and kind, and everyone should help keep them safe and happy.

originalTitle: Iran war live: Tehran says Trump’s peace talk claims are ‘fake news’
title: Let's Talk About Being Friends: How Grown-Ups Can Use Words, Not Worries!
content:
Hello, my wonderful friends from all across Africa! Your friendly news reporter is here to share some very important news with you today. We're going to talk about something super, super important: how grown-ups, just like us, sometimes have big disagreements, and why talking and being honest is the very best way to make things better!

Hello from Africa!

Imagine your classroom. Sometimes, you and your friend might not agree on which game to play, right? Maybe one friend wants to play tag, and another wants to draw. It's a small disagreement, but it's important how you solve it! Do you shout? Or do you talk about it calmly? Grown-ups, even the very important ones who lead countries, sometimes have disagreements too. And just like us, they need to talk nicely and be truthful.

What are we talking about today?

Today, we’re talking about some very important grown-ups from two different places. One of these grown-ups is Mr. Trump, who is the leader of a big country called America. The other grown-ups are from a country called Iran. Now, sometimes, important people try to talk about how they can all be friends and live together peacefully. This is called 'peace talks' – it means sitting down and talking about how to stop any disagreements and be kind. But something a little confusing happened! Mr. Trump said, "Hey, we should talk about being friends!" But then, some important grown-ups from Iran said, "Hmm, we don't think that's true. We don't think he really wants to talk about being friends right now." So, it's a bit like when you say, "I want to share my toys with you!" and your friend says, "No, you don't!" It can be a little puzzling. Did you know? In many parts of Africa, people believe in 'Ubuntu'. It means 'I am because we are'. It teaches us that we are all connected and should help each other!

Why is talking important?

When people have disagreements, the very best thing they can do is talk. Talking helps everyone understand what the other person is thinking or feeling. Imagine if you were upset with your brother or sister, but you just stayed quiet. They wouldn't know why you were sad, right? But if you said, "I feel sad because...", then they could understand and maybe say sorry or try to help. Talking helps grown-ups, too! When leaders of countries talk, they can find ways to solve problems without getting angry or causing more trouble. They can find happy solutions that work for everyone. It's like finding a way for both you and your friend to play tag *and* draw, maybe one after the other! Did you know? There are over 2,000 different languages spoken in Africa! That's a lot of ways to talk and understand each other!

What is Peace?

We talk a lot about 'peace'. But what does it mean? Peace is when everyone is happy, safe, and not fighting. It means countries are friends, and people in those countries are kind to each other. When there is peace, children can go to school, play safely, and families can grow food and live happily without worrying. It's a wonderful, wonderful thing! Did you know? The United Nations has a special day every year called the 'International Day of Peace' on September 21st! On this day, everyone around the world thinks about being kind and peaceful.

Being Truthful is Super Important!

One of the other big ideas in this story is 'telling the truth'. When someone says something is 'fake news', it means they think it's not true, it's made up. It's super important for all of us, especially grown-ups who lead countries, to always tell the truth. If you tell the truth, people trust you. If you don't tell the truth, it's very hard for others to believe what you say, and it makes it much harder to become friends or solve problems. Imagine if your friend always told you stories that weren't true. Would you want to play with them? Probably not, because you wouldn't know what to believe! That's why being honest and truthful is a superpower for making friends and building trust, for kids and for grown-ups! Did you know? A simple handshake is a very old and special way that people show they are friends and want to work together peacefully!

Our Hopes for Tomorrow

As your friendly news reporter from Africa, we always hope for peace. We hope that all the important grown-ups in the world will always choose to talk kindly, listen carefully, and be truthful with each other. Because when grown-ups talk and tell the truth, they can find ways to make the whole world a happier, safer, and more peaceful place for all children, just like you. Let's all remember to talk nicely, be honest, and try to be friends, even when things are a little tricky. Because a kind word and an honest heart can make a big, big difference!
summary: Important grown-ups from America and Iran have a disagreement about whether they are really trying to talk and be friends, reminding us why it's so important for everyone to talk kindly and tell the truth to find peace.
whyMatters: It matters because when grown-ups, especially leaders of countries, talk to each other honestly and kindly, they can solve problems peacefully, which helps everyone, especially children, live in a safe and happy world without worries.
